And, even now, as Kerri confronts the LAYERS of deception, grief, and emotional distress associated with learning the truth of a hidden murderer, publicly, Kerri is encountering unsuspected condemning hurtful angry responses from people who are comfortable with bashing her while hiding under their false veil of living “normal” lives. Kerri, hurt people hurt people. PERIOD. 

Heck, every criminal will attempt to make an honest person feel as if THEY are the criminal. Even in this case, sadly, when Mr. Rader was apprehended, Rader accused the FBI and the leading lieutenant of being liars because of the floppy disk! Like…seriously???

video_title: My Father, the BTK Killer | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
I’m not sure if it’s a coping mechanism but Kerri’s book didn’t sit right with me. 
despite the innumerable hours she spent immersing herself in this case - The articles, news stories, weeks of court transcripts, documentaries, books, and even movies about this case and her father, she doesn’t want anything to do with hearing the personal testimonies from the victims families? That’s the only part of the trial she has never read. She even mentions how upset she was at not having a say in whether or not the victim’s stories should be told in full during her father’s sentencing. She felt her family was a victim as well, and should’ve had an equal say in whether those details should’ve been discussed in full in court.
I’m not saying her family wasn’t a victim to his crimes, they absolutely are - but let's not completely level the playing field here. The victims deserved to have their stories told, they deserved justice, and their families had every right to make that decision.
The entire book came off as a PR piece for her and her family. I’m glad she is sticking up for herself and her family, I’m glad she is telling her story, and taking a part in changing her own narrative - that’s amazing for her. However, in the same breath she is upset that the families of the victims had that right as well - in the time they deserved it most. Why? Why is she so against hearing from the families? Why is it that the victim impact statements are the only transcripts she has never read from the trial? Why was she so against them having their stories told? It doesn’t sit right with me. Sure, every person grieves differently, and she has every right to feel the way she wants to, and heal the way she needs to - but it’s weird she doesn’t see that imbalance.
After all she wouldn’t even be writing a book if it wasn’t for the victims! Not once in this documentary did I hear her say she felt bad for the victims . If it was me I would donate some if not all the money she made from the book to the families that lost a loved one. Just my thoughts!
